content.problemStatement:
In olden days finding square roots seemed to be difficult but nowadays it can be easily done using in-built functions available across many languages 
.


Assume that you happen to hear the above words and you want to give a try in finding the square root of any given integer using in-built functions. So here's your chance.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer N whose integer square root is to be printed.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing ⌊√N⌋, the square root of N rounded down to the nearest integer.

solution.approach: For each N we can directly use the language's standard square‑root routine (e.g., sqrt in C/C++/Java, math.sqrt in Python, Math.sqrt in JavaScript). The result is a floating‑point number; converting it to an integer by truncation (or applying floor) yields ⌊√N⌋ because N is non‑negative. This runs in O(1) per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work for each of the T test cases.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few scalar variables are used.

code.Python3:
import sys, math
data = sys.stdin.read().strip().split()
if not data:
    sys.exit()
T = int(data[0])
out = []
for i in range(1, T+1):
    N = int(data[i])
    out.append(str(int(math.isqrt(N))))
print('\n'.join(out))
